data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-12T00:49:25.841Z</current_datetime>

I have an EF Core 8 app. These two operations are extremely slow because they load all entities into memory:

```csharp
// Operation 1: Deactivate all products not sold in 6 months
var staleProducts = await _db.Products
    .Where(p => p.LastSoldDate < DateTime.UtcNow.AddMonths(-6))
    .ToListAsync();
foreach (var p in staleProducts)
{
    p.IsActive = false;
}
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();

// Operation 2: Delete all audit logs older than 1 year
var oldLogs = await _db.AuditLogs
    .Where(l => l.CreatedAt < DateTime.UtcNow.AddYears(-1))
    .ToListAsync();
_db.AuditLogs.RemoveRange(oldLogs);
await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
```

There are 500K products and 2M audit logs. How do I make these efficient?

data.content:
Use EF Core 7+'s **`ExecuteUpdateAsync`** and **`ExecuteDeleteAsync`** — they translate directly to single SQL statements, bypassing the change tracker entirely:

```csharp
// Operation 1: bulk UPDATE — single SQL statement, zero entities loaded
await _db.Products
    .Where(p => p.LastSoldDate < DateTime.UtcNow.AddMonths(-6))
    .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s =>
        s.SetProperty(p => p.IsActive, false));

// Operation 2: bulk DELETE — single SQL statement, zero entities loaded
await _db.AuditLogs
    .Where(l => l.CreatedAt < DateTime.UtcNow.AddYears(-1))
    .ExecuteDeleteAsync();
```

**Key differences from your current approach:**

| | Old (ToList + SaveChanges) | New (ExecuteUpdate/Delete) |
|---|---|---|
| SQL round-trips | N+1 (SELECT + per-row UPDATE/DELETE) | 1 |
| Memory | All matching entities loaded | Zero entities loaded |
| Change tracker | Engaged | Bypassed |

**Caveats to be aware of:**

- These execute **immediately** — they don't participate in `SaveChangesAsync()` or transactions unless you wrap them in one explicitly.
- The **DbContext cache becomes stale** — any already-tracked entities won't reflect the change. Call `_db.ChangeTracker.Clear()` afterward if you continue using the same context.
- If the affected row count is very large (e.g. 2M deletes), consider **batching** to avoid lock escalation:

```csharp
int deleted;
do
{
    deleted = await _db.AuditLogs
        .Where(l => l.CreatedAt < DateTime.UtcNow.AddYears(-1))
        .Take(10_000)
        .ExecuteDeleteAsync();
} while (deleted > 0);
```

No extra NuGet packages needed — these APIs are built into `Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore` 7+.
data.reasoningText: The user wants to optimize bulk update and bulk delete operations in EF Core 8. They're currently loading all entities into memory, which is extremely slow. EF Core 7+ introduced `ExecuteUpdateAsync` and `ExecuteDeleteAsync` which translate directly to SQL UPDATE/DELETE statements without loading entities.
